concise:
Ayah 1–18 · linked video: 01. Al-Muddaththir (Ayah 1-18)

Surah Al-Muddaththir: Commentary on Ayahs 1-18

Ayah 1

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلْمُدَّثِّرُ

“O you who wraps himself [in clothing]”

This surah marks a pivotal moment in the prophetic mission. While Surah Al-Muzammil focused on the Prophet ﷺ building his spiritual strength through prayer and worship, Al-Muddaththir begins with him rising, now spiritually prepared to undertake his mission.

There has been scholarly discussion about whether this is the first revelation or came after a gap following the initial revelations. The most convincing explanation resolves this by recognizing that after the early revelations in the cave, there was a period of silence. Then Angel Jibreel (AS) appeared again, but this time manifesting himself across the entire horizon. This experience was so overwhelming that the Prophet ﷺ returned home and asked to be covered in a blanket, trembling from what he had witnessed.

The word المدثر specifically refers to someone covering themselves, which is more definitive than المزمل, which means wrapped up. The late Dr. Israr Ahmed offered a compelling analysis: the nubuwwah (prophethood) began with Surah Al-’Alaq, but the risalah (messengership) officially begins here. He ﷺ was already a prophet receiving revelation, but now comes the formal charge to deliver that message to humanity.

Ayah 2

قُمْ فَأَنذِرْ

“Arise and warn”

The command here is قُمْ فَأَنذِرْ - “arise, then warn” - rather than “arise and warn.” This subtle distinction carries profound meaning. The فَ creates a sequence: first rise and do what rising entails, then proceed to warn.

What does rising entail? The previous surah already commanded قُمِ ٱلَّيْلَ - stand in night prayer. So this command connects back to that spiritual preparation. Rise for prayer, establish your worship, complete your spiritual fortification, and once you’ve done justice to that rising, then comes the next stage: warning people.

This structure beautifully captures the Prophet’s ﷺ journey. The standing in prayer, the night vigils, the intimate conversations with Allah ﷻ - all of that is encompassed in قُمْ. Once that foundation is laid, morning comes, and with it the time to go out and begin the warning: فَأَنذِرْ.

Ayah 3

وَرَبَّكَ فَكَبِّرْ

“And your Lord glorify”

This ayah commands وَرَبَّكَ فَكَبِّرْ - specifically and exclusively glorify your Lord. The normal word order in Arabic would be كَبِّرْ رَبَّكَ, but by bringing “your Lord” forward, the ayah emphasizes exclusivity. This isn’t just about declaring Allah’s ﷻ greatness; it’s about declaring that He alone deserves to be glorified.

This carries two critical implications. First, you’re in a society where multiple gods are worshipped. You cannot simply say Allah ﷻ is great or greater - you must declare that He is the only One deserving of takbir. Nobody else can receive this declaration of greatness.

Second, notice the word used is رَبَّكَ - “your Lord” - not just Allah ﷻ. The word Rabb fundamentally establishes a relationship between human beings and Allah ﷻ: the relationship of slave to Master. By commanding “declare the greatness of your Master,” the ayah simultaneously declares that you are His slave, and He is the only Master worthy of worship. This isn’t something limited to ritual prayers or speeches - it’s a comprehensive declaration that He is the only possible Master, and we are all meant to be His slaves.

The verb كَبِّرْ is in the continuous form, not just a one-time declaration. This means continuously, without stopping, declare the greatness of Allah ﷻ alone. And doing this continuously will inevitably offend those who worship other deities or put themselves in positions they don’t deserve.

Ayah 4

وَثِيَابَكَ فَطَهِّرْ

“And your clothing purify”

The command وَثِيَابَكَ فَطَهِّرْ addresses both literal and metaphorical purity. Some scholars say this means literally maintaining clean clothes, establishing physical cleanliness as part of the prophetic character. Others interpret it metaphorically: keep yourself in good company, don’t dirty yourself by compromising the religion, don’t let yourself be stained by the practices of those around you.

Both meanings work together. The external purity of one’s clothing reflects an internal commitment to purity in all aspects of life - in one’s associations, dealings and character.

Ayah 5

وَٱلرُّجْزَ فَٱهْجُرْ

“And uncleanliness avoid”

The word الرُّجْزَ encompasses all kinds of sin, evil deeds, disruption and impurity. فَٱهْجُرْ means to completely abandon, to migrate away from.

Some scholars specifically interpret this as: when people speak to you in ways that provoke anger or upset, get away from that emotion. Don’t let yourself become consumed by anger or frustration. Distance yourself from anything that could disrupt your spiritual state or compromise your mission.

Ayah 6

وَلَا تَمْنُن تَسْتَكْثِرُ

“And do not confer favor to acquire more”

This ayah presents a fascinating grammatical structure. The word مَنَّ has multiple meanings in Arabic. One meaning is to impose your favor on someone - reminding them of what you did for them, making them feel indebted to you. Another meaning is to cut off or discontinue something.

The most compelling interpretation comes from understanding تَسْتَكْثِرُ in its special grammatical form. Notice it’s مرفوع (in the nominative case), not منصوب (accusative), which would make it conditional. As a حال (adverb of state), it describes the condition: “Don’t discontinue while you consider it too much.”

Here’s the profound meaning: Don’t cut off your efforts - your declaring of Allah’s ﷻ greatness, your maintaining purity, your standing and warning - while thinking that the task is too big. Don’t be so overwhelmed by how much work there is that you stop. Don’t let the intimidation of the mountain you have to climb cause you to discontinue your efforts.

In these two words lies the ultimate motivation for anyone doing Islamic work: Keep going even when you don’t see results. Don’t say the world is just getting worse every day - socially, economically, politically - so there’s no hope, what’s the point? The Prophet ﷺ is in a far more hopeless position than we could ever be. He’s alone, receiving these early revelations, being told to stand and warn, without knowing if anyone will listen. Yet he’s commanded: Don’t worry about seeing results. Don’t think about when success will come. Just keep at it.

Ayah 7

وَلِرَبِّكَ فَٱصْبِرْ

“And for your Lord be patient”

The command وَلِرَبِّكَ فَٱصْبِرْ isn’t just “be patient” - it’s “be patient for the sake of your Lord.” Not patience with Allah ﷻ, but patience for His sake, because your Master is commanding you to do this work.

Patience here extends beyond merely enduring hardship. It means staying on task. Don’t let any fluctuation happen in the amount of work you do, the effort you make, the commitment you maintain. Your Master has assigned you this mission - remain steadfast for His sake.

Ayah 8

فَإِذَا نُقِرَ فِى ٱلنَّاقُورِ

“And when the horn is blown”

The transition is sudden: فَإِذَا نُقِرَ فِى ٱلنَّاقُورِ when it is blown into the trumpet. The verb نُقِرَ specifically indicates blowing with sound, not just blowing air. If it were merely blowing air, a different verb would be used.

This tells us something about the nature of the trumpet (الناقور) - the device itself makes a sound, and the angel adds his voice to it. The angel has a voice that he blows into the instrument. This isn’t mentioned casually - it’s a glimpse into the reality of that terrifying moment when the Day of Judgment begins.

Ayah 9

فَذَٰلِكَ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَوْمٌ عَسِيرٌ

“That Day will be a difficult day”

يَوْمٌ عَسِيرٌ - a difficult day. The emphasis on “that day particularly” (يَوْمَئِذٍ) stresses the uniqueness of its difficulty. This isn’t ordinary difficulty - it’s the day when all accounts will be settled, when the truth will be undeniable.

Ayah 10

عَلَى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ غَيْرُ يَسِيرٍ

“For the disbelievers - not easy”

The ayah specifies: عَلَى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ غَيْرُ يَسِيرٍ - upon the disbelievers, not easy at all. While the previous ayah called it difficult, this one emphasizes it won’t be easy in the slightest for those who rejected faith. The double negative construction intensifies the meaning - there will be absolutely no ease for them on that day.

Ayah 11

ذَرْنِى وَمَنْ خَلَقْتُ وَحِيدًا

“Leave Me with the one I created alone”

Now Allah ﷻ speaks directly: ذَرْنِى - Leave Me. We saw this word in the previous surah, and now we see it again. But here it’s ذَرْنِى وَمَنْ خَلَقْتُ وَحِيدًا - Leave Me and the one I created alone.

Picture the scene: interrogators questioning a prisoner, and the enforcer walks in and says, “Leave the room. Leave me with him.” That’s the imagery here - everyone else step back, let Me deal with him Myself.

Who is this person? Someone born an only child (وَحِيدًا), born into wealth, given special care and attention. He grew up with every advantage. This refers to Al-Waleed ibn al-Mughirah, one of the wealthy elite of Makkah. He held tremendous social status, was among the most eloquent poets, and was one of the most intelligent leaders in Makkah.

Significantly, Al-Waleed was among the first to recognize the Quran’s unique nature. He actually heard the Quran and said, “This has a sweetness I have never tasted before.” He wanted to accept Islam. He liked the Prophet ﷺ and didn’t hate him. But he couldn’t leave behind everything that came with accepting Islam.

He was among those who said, “If we were to follow guidance with you, we will be snatched from our land.” Their entire social and economic structure revolved around the idols. Everyone respected them because these idols were there. How could they abandon them? He became one of the pioneer negotiators, trying to work something out with the Prophet ﷺ - the type we read about before who wanted compromise: you give a little, we’ll give a little, let’s work something out.

Allah ﷻ says about him: he knew. He understood the message. On top of that, he was in a position of wealth and status and luxury. Even if he lost some things, he would still have been fine. He wasn’t starving. He wouldn’t lose everything - he’d still have hundreds left even if he lost thousands. He was already in a position of power. But his love of wealth was too strong.

Ayah 12

وَجَعَلْتُ لَهُۥ مَالًا مَّمْدُودًا

“And I granted him extensive wealth”

جَعَلْتُ لَهُۥ - I furnished for him, I provided for him, I made available to him. The verb جَعَلَ means to place something, to make something available. So: I made available to him مَالًا مَّمْدُودًا - wealth that extends.

What kind of wealth? مَمْدُودًا - extended, stretched out. This is wealth that keeps expanding by Allah’s ﷻ blessing (barakah), not on its own. The money itself is already abundant, but it keeps growing. His assets keep increasing.

Ayah 13

وَبَنِينَ شُهُودًا

“And children present [with him]”

وَبَنِينَ شُهُودًا - and sons that he gets to witness all the time. شُهُودًا means witnessed, present. This highlights one of the great blessings Allah ﷻ gave him: not just that he has sons, but that his sons didn’t move away somewhere else. They’re right there in front of him.

Consider how many wealthy people have sons, but their sons don’t live with them. They go away somewhere else. The fathers miss them, want to be with them, but can’t - even their wealth cannot bring their family together. But Al-Waleed? Allah ﷻ gave him money, gave him extended wealth, gave him sons, and the sons stayed with him. He got to see his sons. One of his sons was Khalid ibn al-Waleed (RA), who would later become one of Islam’s greatest military commanders.

Ayah 14

وَمَهَّدتُّ لَهُۥ تَمْهِيدًا

“And spread [everything] before him, easing [his life]”

مَهَّدتُّ لَهُۥ تَمْهِيدًا - I laid down the furnishings for him. The word مَهَّدَ means to lay down a mattress, but metaphorically it means: I laid down all kinds of comfortable arrangements for him. I facilitated things for him in such an extensive way. Everything was made easy, comfortable, accessible.

Ayah 15

ثُمَّ يَطْمَعُ أَنْ أَزِيدَ

“Then he desires that I should add more”

ثُمَّ يَطْمَعُ أَنْ أَزِيدَ - Then, after all of this, he wants that I should increase him even more. Keep giving him wealth. Give him more and more.

Ayah 16

كَلَّآ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ لِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا عَنِيدًا

“No! Indeed, he has been toward Our verses obstinate”

كَلَّا - No! This is a strong repudiation, a heavy rejection. It’s not just “no” (لا) - it’s كَلَّا. When someone makes an empty request that deserves to be shot down firmly, you don’t just say no, you say كَلَّا.

Imagine a child who just failed a math test asking, “Mom, can I have some ice cream?” The response isn’t just “no” - it’s كَلَّا with a metaphorical slap. “You just failed the test!” That’s the weight of this word.

إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ لِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا عَنِيدًا - He was the one who was so stubborn when it came to Our miraculous signs. عَنِيد means obstinate, stubborn. The profound tragedy here is that he accepted the Quran to be true, yet he stubbornly refused to budge from his position of rejection. He knew it was the truth, but his love of worldly position was stronger.

Ayah 17

سَأُرْهِقُهُۥ صَعُودًا

“I will cover him with arduous torment”

سَأُرْهِقُهُۥ صَعُودًا - I will pile onto him a punishment. The word صَعُودًا means climbing, escalating, ascending. So this is a punishment that keeps getting heavier and heavier, escalating rapidly. The punishment itself climbs and intensifies.

أُرْهِقُ means to load something so heavy that nobody has the power to bear it. I will put such a heavy, escalating torment on him that it becomes unbearable.

Ayah 18

إِنَّهُۥ فَكَّرَ وَقَدَّرَ

“Indeed, he thought and deliberated”

إِنَّهُۥ فَكَّرَ وَقَدَّرَ - He thought, no doubt about it, and he made all kinds of calculations.

He gave extensive thought: Should I accept Islam? If that happens, what happens then? How does it all play out? When he was assigned to go and negotiate with the Prophet ﷺ, he strategized: What should I offer him? What will make him change his mind? Based on what I know about him, what could I possibly offer?

This ayah captures the tragedy of someone who intellectually understands the truth but allows worldly calculations to override that understanding. His thinking and planning weren’t in pursuit of truth - they were in pursuit of compromise, of finding a way to avoid the consequences of accepting what he knew to be true.

Ayah 18–42 · linked video: 02. Al-Muddaththir (Ayah 18-42)

Surah Al-Muddaththir: Ayahs 18-42

Ayah 18

إِنَّهُۥ فَكَّرَ وَقَدَّرَ

These ayahs describe a specific incident involving Al-Waleed ibn Al-Mughirah, one of the most eloquent poets of Quraysh. The Quraysh leadership decided to send him to listen to the Quran and deliver a critique that would discourage others from believing in it. He was chosen specifically because of his mastery of the Arabic language and his reputation for eloquence.

Before approaching the Prophet ﷺ, Al-Waleed carefully planned his strategy. The word فَكَّرَ indicates deep, calculated thought, while قَدَّرَ means he planned and measured out his approach. He considered every angle: “The Prophet ﷺ is an orphan, perhaps I can use that in negotiations. Maybe I should offer him power, since he might feel helpless. Perhaps wealth would work, or women, or status.” He was plotting his entire negotiation strategy, thinking through how he would undermine the message of the Quran.

Allah ﷻ exposes this internal scheming because it represents the height of arrogance—to plot against divine revelation with human manipulation.

Ayah 19

فَقُتِلَ كَيْفَ قَدَّرَ

The phrase قُتِلَ literally means “may he be killed,” but it functions here as an expression of condemnation and curse. Allah ﷻ is saying: How dare he make these kinds of calculations? How dare he plot against the truth with such schemes?

This isn’t a literal prayer for his death, but rather an expression of the gravity of his offense. To plot deliberately against divine revelation, to strategize how to reject what you know is truth—this deserves the strongest condemnation.

Ayah 20

ثُمَّ قُتِلَ كَيْفَ قَدَّرَ

The curse is repeated for emphasis: May he be killed again! How could he make these calculations? The repetition intensifies the condemnation, highlighting just how offensive his scheming was to Allah ﷻ. Every step of his manipulation, every calculated move he planned, Allah ﷻ knew it all and found it worthy of this severe rebuke.

Ayah 21

ثُمَّ نَظَرَ

Then he looked. After all his planning and scheming, Al-Waleed came and stared at the Prophet ﷺ as he was reciting the Quran. The word نَظَرَ indicates he was looking intently, studying carefully. This was part of his act—he wanted to appear as though he was genuinely listening with deep concentration.

He knew his reputation preceded him. People knew him as intelligent and thoughtful. So he made sure to look like he was paying very close attention, really considering what he was hearing. This way, when he delivered his verdict, people would think he had given it serious thought and careful consideration.

Ayah 22

ثُمَّ عَبَسَ وَبَسَرَ

Then he frowned and scowled. The word عَبَسَ means he frowned, creating wrinkles on his forehead in apparent concentration. The word بَسَرَ means he became upset, showing displeasure on his face.

This was all theater. He was acting out his disapproval, shaking his head as if to say, “Hmm, let me think about this carefully.” The performance was designed to show doubt and dissatisfaction, all while the Prophet ﷺ was still reciting.

Ayah 23

ثُمَّ أَدْبَرَ وَٱسْتَكْبَرَ

Then he turned his back and showed arrogance. The word أَدْبَرَ literally means he showed his back, he turned away. And اسْتَكْبَرَ means he acted with arrogance and pride.

Picture the scene: after his theatrical display of consideration and disapproval, he literally turned his back on the Prophet ﷺ and walked away with an air of superiority. Allah ﷻ captured every single element of his mind game, every dramatized gesture, because it was so offensive in His sight. Allah ﷻ knew that all of it was calculated, rehearsed in his mind beforehand: “I’m going to do this, then I’ll do that, then I’ll turn around and act like this isn’t the word of Allah.”

Ayah 24

فَقَالَ إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا سِحْرٌ يُؤْثَرُ

So he said: “This is nothing but transmitted magic.” When he returned to the Quraysh leadership, they asked him what he thought. Should they call the Prophet ﷺ a liar? Al-Waleed said no, that won’t work—everyone knows he’s not a liar, that allegation won’t stick.

Should they call him insane? No, that won’t work either. The speech is too coherent, too powerful. An insane person doesn’t speak like this.

Should they call him a fortune teller or mind reader? No, Al-Waleed said, I know how those people speak. They speak in ambiguous terms—“something will happen to you today”—vague statements that could be interpreted either way. This speech is direct, explicit, clear. It’s nothing like what fortune tellers say.

The Quraysh were frustrated: “So what do you want us to say? Are you becoming a believer?” They needed a unified allegation, because if different people said different things, newcomers would think, “You’re all confused about him—let me figure it out for myself.”

Finally, Al-Waleed settled on magic. “Call it سِحْرٌ —magic.” The word يُؤْثَرُ means it’s transmitted, passed down, traced back to other magicians. He’s suggesting that the Prophet ﷺ learned this powerful magic from somewhere.

But notice something critical: magic is the last resort. When you call something magic, you’re acknowledging that it cannot be explained through normal human capacity. You’re admitting it has power that mystifies people, that transforms them in ways you cannot rationally explain. Magic requires belief in the unseen—something you cannot see is causing this effect.

When you called him a liar, you were giving a plausible human explanation. When you called him insane, you were giving a plausible human explanation. But when you call him a magician, you’re saying, “What he has is supernatural. This isn’t humanly possible.” You’re taking a leap of faith—the wrong leap of faith, but a leap nonetheless.

The allegation of magic was already an admission of the Quran’s power. The Prophet ﷺ himself said, “Indeed, in eloquent speech there is magic” (إِنَّ مِنَ الْبَيَانِ لَسِحْرًا). People are mystified by it. They experience complete transformation when they hear it. Look at Umar (RA)—unrecognizable before and after. How do you transform an entire human being’s personality? That’s the real “magic” of this Quran.

Ayah 25

إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا قَوْلُ ٱلْبَشَرِ

“This is nothing but the speech of a human being.” After calling it magic—after acknowledging its supernatural power—Al-Waleed then contradicts himself by saying it’s just human speech. He wants people to know he’s not that impressed. It’s just the words of some mortal, nothing more.

This is the ultimate contradiction: Is it magic or is it human speech? It cannot be both. But this is what happens when people reject truth—they contradict themselves, unable to maintain logical consistency.

Ayah 26

سَأُصْلِيهِ سَقَرَ

“I will cast him into Saqar.” Now Allah ﷻ responds with His judgment. سَقَرَ is one of the names of Hellfire. The word has a linguistic connection to the intense heat that causes one’s head to boil, as when someone has a high fever and walks in the scorching sun.

The structure here uses Saqar as if it were a verb—“I will make him climb” or “I will cast him into” Saqar. It conveys the image of being thrown into this fire, where the heat is so intense that it feels like your head is boiling.

Ayah 27

وَمَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ مَا سَقَرُ

“And what will make you know what Saqar is?” This is a rhetorical question emphasizing the incomprehensible nature of this punishment. You cannot truly grasp what Saqar is. Its reality is beyond human comprehension.

Ayah 28

لَا تُبْقِى وَلَا تَذَرُ

“It neither leaves nor spares.” The word تُبْقِى means it doesn’t allow survival, it doesn’t let you continue. The word تَذَرُ means it doesn’t leave anything behind. It’s relentless, unceasing, giving no respite.

Ayah 29

لَوَّاحَةٌ لِّلْبَشَرِ

“Scorching to the human skin.” The word لَوَّاحَةٌ comes from a root meaning to blacken, like a blackboard. It burns and blackens the skin. لِلْبَشَرِ specifically means “for the skins”—it affects the skin of people directly.

Ayah 30

عَلَيْهَا تِسْعَةَ عَشَرَ

“Over it are nineteen.” Nineteen guardians are over Hellfire. When the disbelievers heard this number, they mocked it. “Only nineteen? That’s less than twenty! That’s just the teens. I could take ten myself—what about you? You can handle nine, right? We’ve got this.”

They turned it into a joke, thinking they could overcome nineteen guardians. This mockery prompted Allah ﷻ to respond in the very next ayah.

Ayah 31

وَمَا جَعَلْنَآ أَصْحَـٰبَ ٱلنَّارِ إِلَّا مَلَـٰٓئِكَةً ۙ وَمَا جَعَلْنَا عِدَّتَهُمْ إِلَّا فِتْنَةً لِّلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لِيَسْتَيْقِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَيَزْدَادَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِيمَـٰنًا ۙ وَلَا يَرْتَابَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ ۙ وَلِيَقُولَ ٱلَّذِينَ فِى قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ وَٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ مَاذَآ أَرَادَ ٱللَّهُ بِهَـٰذَا مَثَلًا ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يُضِلُّ ٱللَّهُ مَن يَشَآءُ وَيَهْدِى مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَمَا يَعْلَمُ جُنُودَ رَبِّكَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۚ وَمَا هِىَ إِلَّا ذِكْرَىٰ لِلْبَشَرِ

“We have made the guardians of the Fire none but angels.” They’re not nineteen people—they’re nineteen angels. One angel is sufficient to destroy an entire town. These are nineteen angels guarding Hellfire. You think you can take them on?

“And We have not made their number except as a trial for those who disbelieve.” The number nineteen itself is a test, a فِتْنَة—something that reveals what’s truly in people’s hearts. Some will mock it, proving their disbelief. Others will investigate it sincerely.

“So that those who were given the Scripture may be certain.” The People of the Book, if they examine their own texts honestly, will find references to nineteen guardians of Hell in their most scholarly and classified scriptures. They’ll realize this man had no access to that knowledge, and it will lead them to conviction about the truth of this revelation.

“And so that those who believe may increase in faith.” When believers see how the Quran confirms previous scriptures in details that couldn’t have been known, their faith strengthens.

“And so that those who were given the Scripture and the believers will not doubt.” Both groups will find their certainty reinforced.

“And so that those in whose hearts is disease and the disbelievers will say, ‘What did Allah intend by this as an example?’” People with spiritual sickness and hardened disbelievers will use the number nineteen as an excuse to question and mock. “What’s the point of mentioning nineteen? What does this number mean?” They’ll go off in strange directions, creating elaborate theories.

This actually happened in recent history. Someone came up with an entire “19 theory,” claiming the Quran is dominated by the number nineteen—that the word “Allah” appears a number of times divisible by nineteen, that every surah has letters divisible by nineteen, and so on. When some parts of the Quran didn’t fit his theory, he actually suggested the Quran must have been changed to fit his calculations. This is exactly what Allah ﷻ warned about: وَمَا جَعَلْنَا عِدَّتَهُمْ إِلَّا فِتْنَةً—the number is a trial, and some will fail that trial by going into absurd tangents.

The ayah itself tells us what “it” refers to in this context. When Allah ﷻ says وَمَا هِيَ —“and what is it?”—the feminine pronoun refers to the closest feminine noun, which is سَقَرَ. The nineteen guardians are over Saqar. The pronoun agreement in Arabic is clear.

“Thus does Allah leave to stray whom He wills and guide whom He wills.” The same information—nineteen guardians—guides some and misguides others, depending on what’s in their hearts.

“And none knows the soldiers of your Lord except Him.” You have no idea what armies your Master has at His disposal. Nineteen angels are just one small battalion. Don’t trivialize Allah’s power.

“And it is not but a reminder for humanity.” The entire passage—the description of Hellfire, the nineteen guardians, all of it—serves as a powerful reminder for people to take seriously.

Ayah 32

كَلَّا وَٱلْقَمَرِ

“No! By the moon.” The word كَلَّا is a strong rejection—“Not at all!” “Absolutely not!” It repudiates what came before. Now Allah ﷻ begins a series of oaths, and when He swears by something, He’s directing our thought process toward a particular realization.

He swears by the moon—a limited source of light in the darkness.

Ayah 33

وَٱلَّيْلِ إِذْ أَدْبَرَ

“And by the night when it retreats.” The word أَدْبَرَ means to turn one’s back, to recede gradually. The night is slowly pulling back, little by little.

Ayah 34

وَٱلصُّبْحِ إِذَآ أَسْفَرَ

“And by the morning when it appears.” The word أَسْفَرَ means to become completely manifest, fully exposed, totally clear. The morning has come in its full brightness.

Notice the progression: First, the limited light of the moon. Then, the night gradually receding. Finally, the morning breaking forth in complete illumination.

This is imagery describing the state of the world when the Prophet ﷺ came. The only revelation remaining was scattered bits and pieces from the Torah and Injil. Some truth was still there, like the moon providing limited light in the darkness. Slowly, as the Quran began to be revealed, that long night—that extended period of darkness over humanity—started to recede.

But it wasn’t going to be just a partial removal of darkness. What was coming? The full-blown morning. Complete illumination. This revelation wouldn’t just add a little light—it would bring the full dawn, removing all darkness, making everything completely manifest.

This is the imagery Allah ﷻ uses to describe the magnitude of what the Quran represents: the end of a long spiritual night and the arrival of a perfect morning.

Ayah 35

إِنَّهَا لَإِحْدَى ٱلْكُبَرِ

“Indeed, it is one of the greatest things.” After those three oaths, Allah ﷻ declares: this revelation, this Quran, is إِحْدَى الْكُبَرِ—among the greatest events that have ever occurred. This is a monumental moment in human history. The coming of this Quran is a massive, transformative event.

Nearly six centuries had passed without a prophet, without revelation. The world was darkening. Some light remained—like the moon—but now it was time for the night to end and for the morning to arrive.

Ayah 36

نَذِيرًا لِّلْبَشَرِ

“As a warning to humanity.” The Quran has come as a نَذِير —a warner—for all people. It warns of consequences, of accountability, of the reality of judgment.

Ayah 37

لِمَن شَآءَ مِنكُمْ أَن يَتَقَدَّمَ أَوْ يَتَأَخَّرَ

“For whoever wills among you to proceed or stay behind.” Now that the morning has come, you have a choice. يَتَقَدَّمَ means to move forward, to take a step ahead toward the light. يَتَأَخَّرَ means to stay back, to remain behind in the darkness.

The choice is yours. The morning has arrived. Do you want to embrace the light? Go ahead. Do you want to stay in darkness? That’s your decision. But understand that the choice has been laid before you clearly.

Ayah 38

كُلُّ نَفْسٍۭ بِمَا كَسَبَتْ رَهِينَةٌ

“Every soul is held in pledge for what it has earned.” This is a profound statement. The word رَهِينَةٌ means collateral, something held as security or a down payment. When you take a loan and can’t provide immediate payment, you give something valuable as guarantee—your watch, a ring, something of worth.

Allah ﷻ is saying: You yourself are an asset being held. Whatever you’ve earned—your deeds—are your collateral. You will be released in exchange for your deeds. Your actions are what determine your fate. Every person is being held accountable for what they’ve acquired in this life.

This is a heavy, weighty reality: you are in custody, and your deeds are what will secure your release or your continued detention.

Ayah 39

إِلَّآ أَصْحَـٰبَ ٱلْيَمِينِ

“Except the companions of the right hand.” These people won’t have to go through that intense interrogation. They won’t be held in the same way. Their path will be made easy. They’ll be shown mercy and gentleness in their accountability.

Who are the companions of the right hand? Those who chose to move forward toward the light, who earned righteous deeds, who responded to the warning.

Ayah 40

فِى جَنَّـٰتٍ يَتَسَآءَلُونَ

“In gardens, asking each other.” The companions of the right hand will be in all kinds of beautiful gardens—جَنَّات, plural, indicating variety and abundance. And what will they do there? يَتَسَآءَلُونَ—they’ll be asking each other questions, having conversations, discussing things.

Ayah 41

عَنِ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ

“About the criminals.” Their questions will be about the مُجْرِمِين—those who committed crimes against themselves and others through their rejection and disobedience. They’ll be asking about what happened to those people.

In this moment, Allah ﷻ will allow them to communicate with those in Hellfire, to ask them directly.

Ayah 42

مَا سَلَكَكُمْ فِى سَقَرَ

“What landed you in Saqar?” This is the question the people of Paradise will ask the people of Hellfire: “What got you into Saqar? How did you end up there? What was it that led you to this?”

The word سَلَكَ means to enter into, to be placed into, to find yourself inserted into something. “What caused you to be put into Saqar?”

This question sets up the following ayahs where the people of Hellfire will respond, listing the reasons for their punishment. But more importantly, this question emphasizes personal responsibility. No one will be in Hellfire by accident or mistake. Each person will be able to identify exactly what led them there—what choices they made, what warnings they ignored, what path they chose when the morning came and they decided to stay behind in darkness.

The entire passage comes full circle: from the scheming of Al-Waleed against the truth, to the warning of Hellfire, to the choice presented to humanity, to the ultimate accountability where everyone will know exactly why they’re where they are.

Ayah 43–56 · linked video: 03. Al-Muddaththir (Ayah 43-56)

Surah Al-Muddaththir: Ayahs 43-56

Ayah 43

قَالُوا۟ لَمْ نَكُ مِنَ ٱلْمُصَلِّينَ

“They will say: We were not from among those who prayed.”

The residents of Hell are asked about what led them to this terrible fate, and their first confession is brutally honest: “We weren’t from the people who prayed.” Notice the emphasis in the Arabic construction here. The word لَمْ itself is a strong negation, but even the noon at the end of نَكُ can be dropped in Arabic to further lighten and emphasize the verb. The grammar itself underscores their complete disconnection from prayer.

What they’re really saying is: “We weren’t the salah type.” This is familiar language even today. When people say, “That’s not really my thing” or “I’m not into that sort of thing,” they’re using the same dismissive attitude. These people in Hellfire will openly admit: prayer just wasn’t who we were. We weren’t among those people.

Ayah 44

وَلَمْ نَكُ نُطْعِمُ ٱلْمِسْكِينَ

“And we did not feed the poor.”

Their second confession follows naturally from the first. Not only did they abandon their vertical relationship with Allah ﷻ through prayer, but they also failed in their horizontal obligations to other human beings. “We weren’t the type to feed the miskeen”—the poor, the helpless, the vulnerable. They weren’t at all from that group of people who felt responsibility toward those in need.

These two failures—neglecting prayer and neglecting the poor—reveal the complete spiritual bankruptcy of these individuals. They had no connection to their Creator and no compassion for His creation.

Ayah 45

وَكُنَّا نَخُوضُ مَعَ ٱلْخَآئِضِينَ

“And we used to engage in vain talk with those who engaged in vain talk.”

Now they describe what they actually did spend their time doing. The word نَخُوضُ literally means to enter into something, but specifically refers to entering into conversations and activities that have no real point or purpose. It’s about immersing yourself in empty, aimless pursuits.

This is their confession: “We used to just talk and chitchat and shoot the breeze aimlessly with people who liked to hang out.” Think about people whose entire lives amount to working during the week and living for the weekend. And what does the weekend involve? Just hanging out with people who also like hanging out. Sitting for hours at bars, spending entire days watching multiple movies back-to-back, going to clubs and parties for hours on end, or playing video games endlessly—now with headphones on, talking trash with strangers online.

This was their life. Bigger and bigger screens, thicker and thicker headphones. Hours upon hours of absolutely nothing meaningful. Just خَوْض —empty immersion in pointless activity.

Ayah 46

وَكُنَّا نُكَذِّبُ بِيَوْمِ ٱلدِّينِ

“And we used to deny the Day of Recompense.”

Their fourth admission strikes at the root of their problem: “We used to consider the Day of Judgment a lie.” The word نُكَذِّبُ means to deem something false, to call something a lie, to treat it as fiction.

“Come on, worried about Judgment Day?” This was their attitude. They dismissed it entirely, mocked those who believed in it, and lived as if accountability would never come.

Ayah 47

حَتَّىٰٓ أَتَىٰنَا ٱلْيَقِينُ

“Until there came to us the certainty.”

They continued in this state until اليقين —the absolute conviction—came to them. What is this certainty? Death itself.

The word يقين in the Quran refers to absolute conviction, the kind of belief that comes from seeing something with your own eyes. “Seeing is believing”—that’s yaqeen. And this is why it’s used for death, because while you might believe or disbelieve in the afterlife, there’s no way to disbelieve in death. It’s all around you. It’s undeniable. It’s the ultimate certainty.

When death arrived, all their denials collapsed. Reality hit them, and there was no more room for pretending.

Ayah 48

فَمَا تَنفَعُهُمْ شَفَـٰعَةُ ٱلشَّـٰفِعِينَ

“Then the intercession of intercessors will not benefit them.”

After describing their crimes and their encounter with death, Allah ﷻ declares that no intercession will help them. Nobody’s pleading on their behalf will matter. No intercessor’s arguments will benefit them at all.

People might claim special status—“I’m from the family of the Prophet ﷺ”—but that won’t work. Even Abu Talib, the uncle of the Prophet ﷺ who loved him and supported him, will not be able to benefit from that relationship. No family connection, no tribal affiliation, no worldly status will provide any protection on that Day.

Ayah 49

فَمَا لَهُمْ عَنِ ٱلتَّذْكِرَةِ مُعْرِضِينَ

“Then what is wrong with them that they turn away from the reminder?”

After describing their fate, Allah ﷻ turns back to address the living. What is wrong with these people? From التذكرة —this ultimate reminder, this profound message—they choose to deliberately ignore it. They overlook it on purpose. They turn away from it معرضين —as those who deliberately avert their gaze.

Ayah 50-51

كَأَنَّهُمْ حُمُرٌ مُّسْتَنفِرَةٌ فَرَّتْ مِن قَسْوَرَةٍۭ

“As if they were frightened donkeys, fleeing from a lion.”

Allah ﷻ gives us a vivid image of their behavior. They’re like wild donkeys—حُمُر —that suddenly start running in terror. Picture those nature documentaries where a lion approaches a herd of zebras. The lion crouches low, stalking its prey. One zebra spots the predator and bolts. The moment the others see one running, they all dash away at full speed, stampeding in wild panic.

This is exactly how these people treat Revelation. One of them sees the Prophet ﷺ coming and thinks, “Oh no, here he comes again. He’s going to start talking about the Quran.” And he makes a run for it. When the others see him running, they all scatter like terrified animals fleeing from a قَسْوَرَة —a lion.

There’s a beautiful story related to this word. A disbeliever once came to the Prophet ﷺ and said, “Your Quran has mistakes.” The Prophet ﷺ asked, “What mistakes?” The man replied, “The Quran doesn’t use proper Arabic words. It says أَسَد (lion) is correct—but your Quran uses قَسْوَرَة.”

The Prophet ﷺ called for Hassan (RA), one of his companions known for his strength. When Hassan (RA) came and sat down, the Prophet ﷺ told him, “Get up and leave.” Hassan (RA) had just arrived but obeyed and got up to leave. Then the Prophet ﷺ called him back: “Come back and sit down.” Hassan (RA) returned and sat. Again, the Prophet ﷺ said, “No, I think you should get up and leave.”

This happened four or five times until Hassan (RA), known for his courage, said in confusion, “O Messenger of Allah ﷺ, what’s happening?” The disbeliever, watching all of this, suddenly understood. Hassan (RA) was called the “lion of the Arabs”—their chief, their strongest warrior. Yet he was instantly obedient, getting up and sitting down at the Prophet’s ﷺ command. The disbeliever realized: this is exactly what the ayah describes—even the bravest running in obedience, just as donkeys flee from a lion. The word قَسْوَرَة wasn’t a mistake; it was precisely chosen.

Who is described as the lion here? The Prophet ﷺ himself and the message he brings.

Ayah 52

بَلْ يُرِيدُ كُلُّ ٱمْرِئٍ مِّنْهُمْ أَن يُؤْتَىٰ صُحُفًا مُّنَشَّرَةً

“Rather, every person among them desires that he would be given scriptures spread out.”

But the reality is even more absurd than their fearful fleeing. Each one of them actually wants revelation to come in their own preferred format. They want صُحُفًا مُّنَشَّرَةً —scrolls and papers that they can spread out and examine for themselves.

“Why does revelation have to come to one person? Why can’t it come to all of us? Let’s see these scrolls ourselves. We’ll do our own audit.” They’re also referring to the scrolls of deeds that are being recorded. They say, “Fine, you claim all our deeds are being written down? That must be a long list. Let’s spread them all out right now and see for ourselves.”

It’s the ultimate arrogance—demanding that Allah ﷻ present His revelation according to their specifications.

Ayah 53

كَلَّا ۖ بَل لَّا يَخَافُونَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةَ

“No! Rather, they do not fear the Hereafter.”

كَلَّا—No! Absolutely not! Allah ﷻ dismisses their demands and excuses. This is all nonsense. The fact of the matter is simple: they don’t fear the Akhirah at all. They have no reverence for it, no concern about it, no genuine worry about facing it. That’s the real issue. If they truly feared what’s coming, none of these excuses would matter.

Ayah 54-55

كَلَّآ إِنَّهُۥ تَذْكِرَةٌ فَمَن شَآءَ ذَكَرَهُۥ

“No! Indeed, it is a reminder. So whoever wills will remember it.”

كَلَّا again—another firm rejection of their attitude. إِنَّهُۥ تَذْكِرَةٌ —This Quran is indeed a powerful reminder, a profound warning.

And then comes the statement of human free will: فَمَن شَآءَ ذَكَرَهُۥ—“Whoever wants, he will remember it. Whoever chooses, he will take heed of it. Whoever wills, he will make the effort to reflect on it and mention it.”

The choice is there. The reminder has been given. What anyone does with it is up to them.

Ayah 56

وَمَا يَذْكُرُونَ إِلَّآ أَن يَشَآءَ ٱللَّهُ ۚ هُوَ أَهْلُ ٱلتَّقْوَىٰ وَأَهْلُ ٱلْمَغْفِرَةِ

“And they will not remember except that Allah wills. He is worthy that He be feared, and He is worthy to forgive.”

But immediately after mentioning human choice, Allah ﷻ reminds us of divine sovereignty: وَمَا يَذْكُرُونَ إِلَّآ أَن يَشَآءَ ٱللَّهُ—“And what will they remember except what Allah ﷻ wills? What will they mention except what is in Allah’s power?”

This is a rare and powerful phrase in the Quran, balancing human responsibility with divine will. They’re powerless without Him. Their ability to remember, to reflect, to turn back—all of it depends ultimately on Allah’s will.

Then comes one of the most beautiful endings in the Quran: هُوَ أَهْلُ ٱلتَّقْوَىٰ —“He is the One deserving of taqwa.” He is the One who deserves that people be conscious of Him, aware of Him, cautious not to disappoint Him. He deserves that we protect ourselves from His displeasure.

The word أَهْل means family or people, but it also means someone who possesses a quality, someone deserving of something, someone qualified for something. When you say لَسْتُ أَهْلاً, you’re saying, “I’m not qualified for this, I’m not deserving of this.”

Allah ﷻ is saying: I am the One qualified to be feared. I am the One who deserves your taqwa.

وَأَهْلُ ٱلْمَغْفِرَةِ—“And He is the One deserving that forgiveness be asked of Him.” Where else will you get maghfirah from? It’s His property. He’s the Owner of it. He’s the only One qualified to grant it. He alone deserves that you turn to Him seeking forgiveness.

This ending is profound because it brings together both fear and hope—the two wings upon which a believer flies. Fear Him, because He deserves to be feared. But seek His forgiveness, because He is the Master of forgiveness.

With this, the surah concludes, leaving us with a choice: Will we be among those who remember, or among those who flee? Will we be among those who seek His forgiveness, or among those who deny until certainty overtakes them?
